title_en: Commentary on Social Ethics, Commerce, and Governance
title_ar: الشرح على الأخلاق الاجتماعية والتجارة والحكم
content_en:
The sections of Sahih Muslim on social ethics, commercial dealings, and governance contain some of the most practically important hadiths in the collection, and an-Nawawi's commentary on them demonstrates his command of fiqh across the schools and his ability to derive practical guidance from complex texts.

The section on the prohibition of riba contains some of the most carefully documented hadiths in Islamic commercial law. An-Nawawi's commentary on the hadith specifying the six commodities subject to riba al-fadl (the prohibition on exchanging like commodities in unequal amounts without delivery on the spot) is among the most nuanced treatments of this topic in the classical literature. He explains the Shafi'i position on what the six commodities share in common — and therefore what other commodities might fall under the same prohibition — while presenting the Hanafi and Maliki positions and their different criteria.

The hadiths on oaths and their fulfillment receive careful treatment. An-Nawawi addresses the question of when a vow (nadhr) becomes binding, what happens when it proves impossible to fulfill, and the specific expiation required for breaking an oath. These are practically important questions, and his commentary navigates them with precision and cross-school awareness.

On governance, the hadiths commanding obedience to Muslim rulers and specifying the limits of that obedience are treated with an-Nawawi's characteristic care. He explains the mainstream Sunni principle that obedience to rulers in permitted matters is obligatory even if the ruler is unjust or corrupt, because the harm of civil strife generally exceeds the harm of unjust governance. At the same time, he notes the clear limit: obedience is not due when the ruler commands something forbidden by Allah, and in such cases refusal is obligatory.

The sections on knowledge and teaching contain hadiths about the obligation to seek and transmit knowledge that an-Nawawi treats with evident personal investment. His commentary on the hadith about the three things that continue to benefit a person after death — ongoing charity, beneficial knowledge, and a righteous child who prays for the parent — is among the most widely cited passages in Islamic scholarship on the value of learning.
